subject: Dermatology
question: A 50-year-old diabetic man presents with a red, warm, swollen, tender area on his right lower leg with poorly demarcated borders. Temperature 38.5°C. What is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?

options.explanation: Cellulitis presents as spreading erythema with poorly defined borders, warmth, tenderness, and systemic symptoms. Most common organisms: S. pyogenes (GAS) and S. aureus. Treat with cephalexin 500mg QID or clindamycin. Cover MRSA if risk factors present.
Erysipelas has WELL-DEMARCATED raised borders, bright red, involves superficial lymphatics (face common). Cellulitis borders are poorly defined.
DVT presents with unilateral swelling/pain but without erythema and warmth unless phlegmasia. This has fever and redness suggesting infection.
Contact dermatitis is pruritic, not painful. No fever or systemic symptoms. Well-demarcated borders matching allergen contact pattern.
NF presents with severe pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, rapid progression, and systemic toxicity. This is mild cellulitis without these features.

educationalObjective: Diagnose cellulitis by spreading erythema + poorly defined borders + systemic symptoms. Treat with cephalexin or clindamycin.
keyTakeaway: Cellulitis: poorly demarcated, warm, tender, erythematous. Treat with anti-strep/staph antibiotics. Mark borders to monitor progression.
